reasoning: The informal statement says P takes "values ±1 at three different integer points," meaning at three distinct integer points the values each lie in {+1, -1} in any combination. The Lean hypothesis fixes a specific pattern: P(a) = 1, P(b) = -1, P(c) = 1 — exactly two values are +1 and one is -1. This excludes valid instances of the informal hypothesis, e.g., polynomials where all three distinct integer points have value +1 (like P(x) = (x-1)(x-2)(x-3) + 1, which satisfies P(1)=P(2)=P(3)=1) or all have value -1, or the pattern (+1, -1, -1). The standard pigeonhole proof (using that a-z, b-z, c-z divide ±1, hence are in {±1}, but are 3 distinct values) works for all ±1 configurations, so the informal claim is genuinely more general. Thus Informal ⟹ Lean but Lean ⇏ Informal, making the Lean statement strictly weaker. This is not a case of surfacing an implicit premise — the informal statement is unambiguously true in full generality.
